main_summary.summary: Aaron Dixon, founding captain of the Black Panther Party's Seattle chapter, recorded this interview in Seattle, Washington, on May 11, 2013, with interviewer David Cline for the Civil Rights History Project. Dixon traces his family's migration from Mississippi and Kentucky to Chicago and then to Seattle's Madrona neighborhood in 1957-58, his politicization through marching with Martin Luther King Jr. as a teenager and hearing Stokely Carmichael speak in Seattle, and the founding of the University of Washington Black Student Union. Following the April 4, 1968 assassination of King while Dixon was jailed for the Franklin High School demonstration, he and his brother Elmer met Bobby Seale at Little Bobby Hutton's funeral in Oakland and established the Seattle Black Panther Party chapter, the first BPP office outside California. Dixon details the chapter's armed confrontations with Seattle police, the breakfast for school children program at Madrona Presbyterian Church, the first sickle-cell anemia mass testing at Walla Walla State Penitentiary, Mayor Wes Uhlman's refusal to allow an ATF raid, his 1972 relocation to Oakland as bodyguard to Elaine Brown during the Bobby Seale mayoral campaign and Lionel Wilson's successful mayoral run, and the party's decline under Huey Newton's return.
key_themes: Black Panther Party Seattle chapter founding and operations
BPP community survival programs (breakfast, medical clinics, food bank, sickle-cell testing)
FBI COINTELPRO and assassination attempts on chapter leadership
BPP entry into electoral politics and Oakland municipal power
main_summary.historical_significance:
Dixon's testimony documents the BPP's national expansion from a Bay Area organization into a coast-to-coast network, with Seattle as the first chapter outside California. His first-person account establishes specific operational details rarely captured elsewhere: the Bobby Seale-led founding meeting at his parents' Seattle home, the two-weapons-and-two-thousand-rounds membership threshold, the 25-book required reading list, the 1968 Rainier Beach High School armed intervention, and Mayor Wes Uhlman's blocking of the ATF raid that had destroyed the Chicago and Los Angeles chapters.

The interview also provides a participant's view of internal BPP dynamics often obscured in published accounts: the 1969 purges, the East Coast/West Coast Newton-Cleaver split, Elaine Brown's tenure as chairperson, the party's transition from armed cadre to electoral machine that elected Oakland's first Black mayor Lionel Wilson, and Newton's post-exile destabilization of the organization through cocaine and extortion of after-hours clubs. Dixon's candor about the criminal involvement, embezzlement conviction, and FBI fugitive year that followed his BPP departure adds primary-source detail to the post-BPP biographies of rank-and-file leadership.

chapters.title: Interview Opening and Framing
chapters.summary: Interviewer David Cline opens the May 11, 2013 Seattle session for the Civil Rights History Project, noting the Library of Congress and Smithsonian NMAAHC sponsorship and cameraman John Bishop, then invites Aaron Dixon to begin with the family into which he was born.

chapters.title: Maternal Line Out of Mississippi
chapters.summary: Dixon traces his mother's side from a formerly enslaved ancestor born in 1867 through his great-great-grandmother Emma, who could find work only at the laundry under Jim Crow, and recounts his grandmother's mixed parentage with a Jewish father and her mother's ostracism in Mississippi.

chapters.title: Roy Sledge and a Mother's Music
chapters.summary: Dixon describes his grandparents Roy Sledge, a part-Native American blacksmith's son who worked as a Pullman Porter and battled alcoholism before retiring from the post office, and Josephine, and notes his mother became a child piano prodigy who graduated high school at sixteen.

chapters.title: Father's Kentucky Roots and Brooksville
chapters.summary: Dixon turns to his father's Kentucky family: a plantation inherited through a slaveholder relationship, two great-great-uncles among the first Black soldiers to volunteer for the Union who received land and founded the town of Brooksville, and a family line some traced back to Scottish royalty.

chapters.title: Father Trains as an Artist
chapters.summary: In Chicago, Dixon's grandfather Elmer worked for a wealthy Jewish man and kept his wife from having to work, while Dixon's father won a young scholarship to the Art Institute, captained the ROTC, and rushed to enlist after Pearl Harbor only to be told the service did not take boys like him.

chapters.title: Drafted Into a Segregated Army
chapters.summary: Dixon recounts his father being drafted and sent to Mississippi, where conditions grew so dangerous that he wrote his mother and she in turn wrote Eleanor Roosevelt. He relates the story of a white farmer who pointed a shotgun to bar the Black soldiers from crossing his field, and the barn they burned that night in response.

chapters.title: A Sheriff's Threat in the Deep South
chapters.topic: Father's Wartime Danger in the South
chapters.summary: Dixon tells of his father insisting on walking a light-skinned woman home through a Southern town in uniform, drawing a town sheriff's warning not to be there when he returned, and stopping at the post office to write his mother that his life was in danger before making it back to base.

chapters.title: Okinawa, AWOL, and a Changed Man
chapters.summary: After a barracks riot over a canceled furlough, Dixon's father shipped to the Pacific, fought in the Philippines and Okinawa, and nearly shot a Marine who mutilated a dead Japanese woman. He went AWOL, sheltered with a Japanese family, and returned home with his view of the nation transformed, drawn toward Paul Robeson's youth brigade and the Communist Party.

chapters.title: From Champaign to Seattle
chapters.summary: Dixon's parents married, had children, and his father worked as a technical illustrator at Chanute Air Force Base in Champaign, Illinois, where men in black suits Dixon assumed were FBI once came to the door. A Boeing job offer drew the family to Seattle in 1957 or 1958, a city his father remembered fondly from the war years.

chapters.title: Seattle's Wartime Black Community
chapters.summary: Dixon and Cline discuss how Seattle's Black population grew during the war as people came to work at Todd Shipyards and the airplane factories, settling in the Central Area alongside Chinese, Filipino, Japanese, and Native American neighbors, before the Dixons bought a house in the Madrona neighborhood.

chapters.title: Growing Up in Madrona
chapters.summary: Dixon paints Madrona and its neighboring districts (Madison Valley, Leschi, Mount Baker) as a beautiful, multiracial enclave of big houses where Black, Filipino, Japanese, and Chinese families went to school together and Chinese-owned grocers extended credit, while his mother still faced employment discrimination, notably at Frederick & Nelson.

chapters.title: A Household of Art and Music
chapters.summary: Dixon describes a home shaped by his father's love of other cultures: a multiethnic folk-dancing group that met at their house, a grandmother in Chicago who required every child to play an instrument, and exposure to Shakespeare, opera, jazz, classical music, and Paul Robeson recordings. His father showed his artwork at the Frye Museum.

chapters.title: Sports and the Racism Outside the Cocoon
chapters.summary: Dixon recalls playing football, baseball, basketball, and tennis at the park across the street, his brother Michael earning a UCLA tennis scholarship, and the racial tension when their teams played in Ballard and Queen Anne, neighborhoods Black youth could not safely enter. The cocoon of Madrona always had racism pressing at its edges.

chapters.title: Stories at the Kitchen Table
chapters.summary: Race consciousness came through oral tradition rather than direct instruction, with stories from grandparents and great-grandparents whenever the family was back in Chicago. Dixon recounts his parents' anger when he wanted to be a policeman at thirteen and to join the Marines for Vietnam at sixteen, his father drawing on his own military experience.

chapters.title: Marching With King, Working to Be Independent
chapters.summary: Around twelve or thirteen Dixon marched with Martin Luther King Jr. during King's Seattle visit and demonstrated when George Wallace came to town, possibly drawn in through his church. He recounts working long hours from his junior year, buying his own clothes and records, determined to be independent, and graduating high school in 1967 by the skin of his teeth.

chapters.title: Stokely Carmichael Lights the Fuse
chapters.summary: Turned down by Tennessee State, Dixon was fired up when Stokely Carmichael came to Seattle, started wearing the same glasses, and grew in his understanding of America's racial politics against a backdrop of the 1965 Los Angeles riots, the yearly summer uprisings, and the assassination of John F. Kennedy that had made his father weep.

chapters.title: Ray-Bans, Theater, and a Walked-Off Job
chapters.summary: Newly radicalized, Dixon blew up at a supervisor who told him to remove his Ray-Bans and walked off a job he had held three years. He found part-time work performing racial skits for a poverty program, joined playwright Aaron Dumas's play General's Coup about a Black army seizing the country, and deepened his own poetry writing.

chapters.title: Into the University of Washington
chapters.summary: A poetry reading won Dixon a five-hundred-dollar scholarship, and through an Urban League college program run by a Jewish woman named Mrs. Richmond he scored well on the SAT and entered the University of Washington, arriving as the movement around him was catching steam.

chapters.title: Founding the UW Black Student Union
chapters.summary: Dixon helped start the first Black Student Union at the University of Washington, meeting in the HUB as Ron Dellums and San Francisco State organizers came up amid the San Francisco State takeover, and the group helped form a BSU at Garfield High School. He first heard of the Black Panther Party's 1967 march on the Sacramento capitol around this time.

chapters.part: From Student Protest to the Panthers
chapters.title: The Franklin High School Demonstration
chapters.summary: Over a racial incident in which a Black student was expelled while a white student stayed, and a lack of Black or Asian staff, the BSU marched into Franklin High School, drove the principal out, and rallied the students in the auditorium. A week later Dixon was arrested at home for unlawful assembly.

chapters.part: From Student Protest to the Panthers
chapters.title: King's Assassination in a Jail Cell
chapters.topic: MLK Assassination and Turn to Arms
chapters.summary: On April 4, 1968, while Dixon sat in the King County jail, Walter Cronkite announced that Martin Luther King Jr. had been assassinated and riots broke out across the country. That night Dixon resolved that his picket sign would be replaced by a gun, an idea already planted through earlier organizing with SNCC and a contact who kept guns.

chapters.part: From Student Protest to the Panthers
chapters.title: Little Bobby Hutton's Funeral
chapters.summary: At the Black Student Union conference in San Francisco, Dixon went to the funeral of slain Panther Little Bobby Hutton, stopping for berets to fit in, and saw Marlon Brando out front talking with Bobby Seale. Inside, Hutton's mother wailed over the body as Panthers lined the walls with arms folded.

chapters.part: From Student Protest to the Panthers
chapters.title: The Shootout That Killed Hutton
chapters.summary: Dixon recounts the circumstances of Hutton's death: after King's assassination, Eldridge Cleaver led some Panthers into a confrontation with police while Huey and Bobby were jailed. Cornered in an abandoned house and tear-gassed, Hutton surrendered and was told to run, then shot some twenty-five times, while Cleaver was wounded and David Hilliard and eighteen others were arrested.

chapters.part: From Student Protest to the Panthers
chapters.title: Asking Bobby Seale for a Chapter
chapters.summary: After Bobby Seale's emotionally charged speech that evening, Dixon and his brother made a beeline to him and asked for a Black Panther Party chapter in Seattle. Dixon reflects on why the radical Black Student Union invited the party, given the BPP's growing influence and its central role in the San Francisco State strike through figures like Emory Douglas and George Murray.

chapters.part: From Student Protest to the Panthers
chapters.title: The Founding Meeting in His Parents' Home
chapters.summary: Bobby Seale and George Murray came to Seattle and met with about twenty prospective members over two or three days at the Dixon family home. Seale set the membership bar at two weapons and two thousand rounds of ammunition, assigned a twenty-five-book reading list and two hours of daily study, and the group pointed at Dixon, nineteen and still a student, as their captain.

chapters.title: Summoned to Oakland in Uniform
chapters.summary: A week later Dixon was called to Oakland by the Central Committee, taking his first airplane flight in the full Panther uniform: black leather jacket, powder blue shirt, black beret and boots. Picked up by Vietnam veterans Robert Bay and Tommy Jones, he met the National Secretary and was taken to the home of captains Landon and Randy Williams, Special Forces veterans who showed off their armament.

chapters.title: Bitter Dog and the Shot-Out Television
chapters.summary: Dixon visited Huey in jail, sold papers, met Stokely Carmichael and Don Cox at a Central Committee meeting where he was introduced as a new captain, then drank the street concoction later dubbed Panther Piss and smoked at the captains' house, where Landon shot out the television because the Indians always got killed in the Cowboys and Indians shows.

chapters.title: Baptism at Seventh and Wood
chapters.summary: Two weeks after Hutton's death, Dixon and young Panther Orlando Harris cursed a passing police car, drawing reinforcements to Seventh and Wood, the same corner where Huey Newton's shootout had happened. The Panthers spread out armed as a tense standoff built, broken only when Landon tripped over a garbage can lid and the police silently drove off. Dixon calls it his baptism into the party.

chapters.title: Named First Captain Outside California
chapters.summary: At the Saturday Panther meeting at St. Augustine's Church, with about 125 members from across the Bay Area, Bobby Seale taught the Ten-Point Program and introduced Dixon as first captain of the Seattle chapter, the first BPP office outside California. A false alarm sent them speeding back to the church armed before Dixon flew home to begin organizing.

chapters.title: Answering the Community's Calls
chapters.summary: The Seattle chapter opened its office and took over 300 applications in a month, carrying legally and illegally acquired guns openly through the streets. The community began calling with domestic violence and landlord disputes, including a case where Panthers carried a removed door back and reattached it for a woman with seven children, until Bobby Seale ordered them to cut back.

chapters.title: Reading, Discipline, and the Lumpen
chapters.summary: Dixon describes the party's political education: Mao's Red Book, the three rules and seven points, Fanon, Black Skin White Masks, and Black Rage, plus James Baldwin and Richard Wright. He explains how the required study lifted even Bobby Hutton to literacy, and how a combination of lumpen proletariat and college students came together in the membership.

chapters.title: Armed Intervention at Rainier Beach
chapters.summary: After repeated pleading calls from mothers whose children were being beaten by bat-wielding white students at Rainier Beach High School, thirteen armed Panthers went out, kept their rifles legally unloaded by the chamber rule, and confronted the principal, warning him to protect the children. Dixon calls it the first major incident marking the chapter's emergence.

chapters.title: Robberies, Guns, and the First Purge
chapters.summary: Dixon acknowledges members who joined for personal reasons and began robbing banks and stores, the same pattern emerging in Oakland because the party could not provide income. From prison Huey ordered a purge; both Oakland and Seattle expelled people, an upheaval that cost two lives and badly damaged the party's credibility.

chapters.title: The U.N. Delegation and the SNCC Break
chapters.summary: Dixon recounts the July plan for Bobby Seale, Eldridge Cleaver, and Emory Douglas to bring a free-Huey demonstration to the United Nations, with the SNCC coalition meant to provide entry through James Forman. A meeting at the SNCC office erupted into a near fistfight, the coalition died on the spot, and they turned instead to Mae Mallory for a small audience with a tenth-floor delegation.

chapters.title: The Typewriter Setup and Seattle's First Riot
chapters.summary: Back in Seattle, Dixon was arrested with Curtis Harris on a charge of stealing a typewriter, a setup arranged by an informer who had lured them to take it while plainclothes detectives watched. A Garfield High School rally and march on the jail erupted into Seattle's first major riot, three days of fire-bombing and overturned police cars, and Dixon was released at midnight.

chapters.title: Pork Chop Hill and the Summer of Sniping
chapters.summary: Released into an ongoing riot, Dixon joined armed Panthers heading toward Garfield until a helicopter spotlight, shot out by a Panther, drove them into a sympathizer's house as police surrounded the area. The standoff escalated into a sniping and fire-bombing campaign, with Madrona Hill nicknamed Pork Chop Hill after the Korean War battle, lasting through the summer of 1968.

chapters.title: The Metro Bus Standoff
chapters.summary: Dixon explains the Panthers refused to negotiate with police but met a Seattle Metro delegation over bus parking: when a driver tested them by parking in front of the office during a meeting, Elmer and another Panther boarded and threw the driver's keys onto the roof. Dixon turned himself in on the resulting charge and a Black judge let him off.

chapters.title: First Casualties of the Chapter
chapters.summary: In the fall of 1968 the Seattle chapter lost its first member, a seventeen-year-old shot in the back by police while defending his mother, a part-time Panther and hustler. A December casualty followed when a member was sent to rob a grocery store and was killed by the owner, signaling to Dixon that something hidden was being orchestrated against them.

chapters.title: Acquitted, and the Two Camps Debate
chapters.summary: At the December trial the prosecution's secret witness never appeared and Dixon was found not guilty. After another purge, the chapter opened its first breakfast program at Madrona Presbyterian Church in the winter of 1969, sparking debate within the party between those committed to armed guerrilla warfare, championed by Eldridge Cleaver, and those, like Bobby Seale, who favored community programs.

chapters.title: Hoover's Three Targets and a Justice Department Warning
chapters.summary: With Bobby Seale kidnapped and tried in Chicago and Huey in prison, the party came under attack. Dixon recounts Hoover's vow to eliminate the Panthers by 1969, the January assassinations of Bunchy Carter and John Huggins and the December killing of Fred Hampton, and a Black Justice Department official from Seattle who tipped the chapter off that a raid meant to kill them was coming.

chapters.title: Sandbags and the Survival Programs
chapters.summary: The chapter moved into a community-center duplex, fortified it with sandbags and steel, and spent the brothers' final 1,700-dollar financial aid checks on guns and protective gear. Alongside defense they expanded to five breakfast locations and started Seattle's first free medical clinic, first food bank, free legal aid, and a bussing-to-prisons program with a donated 35-passenger bus.

chapters.title: An Intense Communal Life
chapters.summary: Married in 1968 with a son born in 1969 and a wife in the party, Dixon describes selling a hundred papers a day, running early-morning breakfast programs with no government funding, eating communally, attending weapons and education classes, and pulling two-hour security shifts through the night, a constant, fearless, high-energy lifestyle with only twenty to twenty-five members doing the work of hundreds.

chapters.title: Wes Uhlman Blocks the ATF Raid
chapters.summary: After raids killed Fred Hampton in Chicago and besieged the bunkered Los Angeles office until the Panthers ran out of ammunition, the ATF came to Seattle, but Mayor Wes Uhlman refused to allow it, citing the chapter's deep community ties and its sickle-cell testing, including the first mass testing at Walla Walla State Penitentiary. Dixon notes the city had only one Black officer.

chapters.title: Nixon's Enemy List and a Bounty
chapters.topic: Nixon's Enemy List and the Bounty
chapters.summary: Dixon describes how other mayors followed Uhlman in refusing raids, how Uhlman was frozen out at the U.S. Conference of Mayors under Nixon, and his own place on Nixon's enemy list. The Seattle Police put a 25,000-dollar contract on his head during the summer of 1968, when Time magazine ranked the city first in fire-bombings and second in sniping, hoping his removal would end the campaign.

chapters.title: Stolen Dynamite and an Escape in the Dark
chapters.summary: Dixon recounts how dynamite the chapter had obtained vanished, taken by police informants, and how a couple of days later those informants and Curtis Harris offered to walk him home, then fired at a passing police car and disappeared, leaving him to flee through backyards. A man who knew his family pulled him into a house just as police searched the area.

chapters.title: The Exploding Shotgun Rigged to Kill
chapters.summary: In the fall of 1971, test-firing the chapter's weapons, Dixon raised his riot-18 shotgun to his shoulder, then an instinct told him to lower it. As the firing pin struck, the gun exploded and blew his arm halfway off, leaving him with a steel rod, two plates, and four surgeries. Tests showed the gunpowder had been replaced with high explosives, another assassination attempt; he was back at the office within three days.

chapters.title: The Newton and Cleaver Split
chapters.summary: Dixon describes Huey's release, the FBI letters fostering distrust between Newton and Cleaver, and the rupture when the New York Panthers chose to take orders from the Weather Underground and Newton expelled them. As officials were summoned to Oakland to declare sides, most of New York and New Jersey went with Cleaver, fighting broke out in the streets, and Newton chose not to escalate into all-out war.

chapters.title: Centralization and Elmer's Pardon
chapters.summary: To strengthen Oakland as a base, the centralization campaign brought Panthers from across the country. With his brother Elmer imprisoned in Oregon over a leather-coat robbery, Dixon, his father, and his attorney won a first-of-its-kind gubernatorial pardon through a petition drive before relocating to Oakland, leaving a skeleton crew in Seattle that kept the clinic and programs running.

chapters.title: Ten Thousand Bags of Groceries
chapters.summary: In Oakland the party entered electoral politics, first backing Shirley Chisholm's presidential run, then mounting the Bobby Seale for Mayor and Elaine Brown for City Council campaign. Five offices and a kickoff at the Oakland Auditorium gave away 10,000 grocery bags, each with a chicken still frozen the next day; Dixon recalls the floor covered with empty bags afterward.

chapters.title: Sidelined as Officer of the Day
chapters.summary: Confined to the office as officer of the day, Dixon felt Newton was trying to get rid of him by keeping him off the security squad, telling others Dixon had too much power and commanded too much respect in the party. Dixon notes Newton ran off many good people the same way.

chapters.title: Building a Political Machine
chapters.summary: Though Bobby Seale's mayoral bid fell short, Dixon explains how close it came and how it built a genuine political machine, registering and turning out hundreds of voters, many of them elderly people who had never voted, with vans carrying people from their homes to the polls. Politicians began coming to the party because it could deliver the vote.

chapters.title: Never Wedded to One Tactic
chapters.summary: Dixon reflects on the party's adaptability, moving from armed revolution to survival programs to electoral politics, and its broad coalitions with the Brown Berets, Red Guard Party, American Indian Movement, Young Patriots, SDS, and Peace and Freedom Party, even as the left criticized both the coalitions and the survival programs as insufficiently revolutionary.

chapters.title: The Oakland Community School and the Lamp Post
chapters.summary: Dixon catalogs Elaine Brown's rebuilding: the Oakland Community School ranked among the country's best, a martial arts program on the cover of Black Belt Magazine, the Bay Area's first gang intervention program funded by a CETA grant, a seniors-against-a-fearful-environment escort program, the Lamp Post restaurant, a promotion business bringing Marvin Gaye and Chaka Khan to Oakland, and a portfolio of property.

chapters.title: Drugs, Extortion, and the Nation of Islam
chapters.topic: Drugs and the Nation of Islam
chapters.summary: Dixon describes a contentious relationship with the Nation of Islam tied to Huey's drug operation, recounting Newton's meeting with Louis Farrakhan dividing San Francisco and Oakland, his ban on heroin but tolerance of cocaine, and the policy of forcing after-hours clubs and pimps to pay the party, along with a movie theater acquired from the Mafia.

chapters.title: Elaine Brown at the Height of Power
chapters.summary: Dixon describes Elaine Brown becoming one of the most powerful figures in California, courted by Leo McCarthy and Willie Brown, cultivating corporations that would write 400,000-dollar checks for the school, and securing six judgeships from Governor Jerry Brown by naming six Black attorneys, giving the party real influence over Oakland's judiciary.

chapters.title: Electing Oakland's First Black Mayor
chapters.summary: Dixon recounts running Lionel Wilson's campaign and putting the first Black mayor into office in Oakland, then placing two Panther women on the port commission while a mostly-women Central Committee effectively ran the city. He describes the shift from the Panther uniform to suits after the 1969 order to stop dressing in ways that made them targets.

chapters.title: Felix Mitchell and the Redevelopment Fight
chapters.summary: Dixon recounts Elaine Brown becoming president of the Oakland Redevelopment Corporation, forcing in 1,000 low-income housing units and getting Jerry Brown to release freeway-ramp funds, even as the chapter mobilized against Felix Mitchell's well-armed drug gang that had taken over a housing project near the school, a confrontation Dixon expected to turn bloody.

chapters.title: Newton's Return and the Party's End
chapters.topic: Newton's Return and the Party's Collapse
chapters.summary: Dixon describes Elaine Brown paying Huey's way back from Cuba to stand trial as a fatal mistake: Newton wanted her money and power, forced her out, resumed cocaine, and ordered a trial witness eliminated. The botched hit killed a Panther instead, whose body and abandoned M-16s surfaced in the Oakland Tribune, spiraling the party downhill to its effective end.

chapters.title: Dumping the Arsenal in the Bay
chapters.summary: As a congressional investigation convened, Dixon's last assignment was to destroy the party's illegal weaponry. He spent two days crawling through crawl spaces and attics gathering machine guns, fifty-caliber and anti-aircraft weapons, M-16s, and AK-47s accumulated over the years, and dumping them off the Marin bridge at three in the morning in the longest U-Haul he could rent.

chapters.title: Leaving on His Own Terms
chapters.summary: After a confrontation with Bill Cosby in which he had no defense for Huey, Dixon left the party, later warned that Newton had planned to have him killed. Unlike most who slipped away at night, Dixon arranged a year's leave of absence through Big Bob Heard, reflecting that none of them, with over thirty members killed between 1967 and 1972, had ever expected to live long.

chapters.title: Cocaine, Embezzlement, and a Year on the Run
chapters.summary: Unable to imagine returning to college after life on the other side of the law and addicted to the adrenaline, Dixon got involved in the criminal life, selling cocaine and committing embezzlement that earned 100,000 dollars and a year as an FBI fugitive. He was eventually caught, served about a year, and came out determined to put his life back together.

chapters.title: Becoming the Best Counselor on Staff
chapters.summary: Dixon describes becoming a substance-abuse counselor in Oakland despite never having used drugs himself, winning over skeptical clients through a natural gift for empathy and bringing Panther-style programs into the work, until an evaluation meeting where the controlling executive director of the West Oakland Health Center insulted him and Dixon cursed him out and walked off the job.

chapters.title: The Crack Epidemic Hits Chocolate City
chapters.summary: After a stint on a Northern California marijuana farm, Dixon returned to Seattle as the crack cocaine epidemic struck, the subject of his planned next book. He describes watching it descend like a black cloud on Oakland, the vibrant 1970s Chocolate City with its Black mayor and nightclubs, devastating families, friends, and former party members, then following him to Seattle a month later.

chapters.title: Nonprofit Work and a Finished Book
chapters.summary: Dixon describes the nonprofit he started about ten years earlier: transition housing for 18-to-24-year-olds, a youth leadership project across five schools, Seattle's first high-school media literacy program, and trips with youth to a farm in Brazil. Burned out and a poor administrator who fell behind on taxes, he separated from the organization in August and released his book on September 17.

chapters.title: Why Things Got Worse
chapters.summary: Asked about his legacy, Dixon says that although the health clinic survives, police brutality and community conditions are far worse because the unity among Black and working-class people is gone. He attributes the decline to the individualism, narcissism, and materialism pushed since the 1970s, Reagan-era neoliberalism and deregulation, and the Reagan-Bush cocaine pipeline into Black communities.

chapters.title: Mass Incarceration and a Lost Value System
chapters.summary: Dixon points to two and a half million people in prison, disproportionately Latino and Black men, and the crack addiction that destroyed families and community. He contrasts this with the cultural value system of his own two-parent upbringing, where families stayed together for the children, a sacrifice and commitment he says no longer exists.

chapters.title: Closing: A Packed House at the UW
chapters.summary: Dixon closes by describing a busy two weeks of speaking events in Seattle, including a well-attended University of Washington gathering where he brought Elaine Brown and Cha-Cha Jiménez of the Young Lords, and affirms he remains close with Elaine and Ericka Huggins, before the program's standard Library of Congress and Smithsonian sign-off.

loc_video.caption: Aaron Dixon oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Seattle, Washington, 2013 May 11
